steps.text: Determine the operational limits and within-laboratory performance attributes of the analytical method.
Consult the scientific literature for descriptions of similar previously validated methods to obtain examples of relevant experimental designs.
Demonstrate specificity through the use of pure positive and negative control cultures.
Perform studies to determine whether QC material recoveries and responsiveness to interferences are representative of the true analyte.
Ensure that control material does not interfere with the detection of the true analyte at low concentration.
Prepare a complete written description of the method.
Include the numerical and descriptive specifications of the method’s operational limits and performance attributes in the written method.
Conduct single-laboratory testing in the matrix types for which the method will be used (for Tier 1 validation).

Provide detailed description of method performance, including data on precision, bias, detection limits (including the method by which they were determined and matrices to which they apply), statistical procedures used to develop performance specification, etc.
Provide a side-by-side comparison of performance versus reference method specifications.
Describe aspects of this method that minimize reagents or prevent pollution that may be attributable to the reference method.
Cite how waste and samples are minimized and properly disposed.
Conduct a readiness review of each participating laboratory to ensure compliance with EPA's Quality Assurance Project Plan 2003-01.

Provide the method protocols, standardized test forms, data sheets, media preparation sheets, selected media and reagents, test chemicals, inoculated porcelain penicylinders, and test parameters to each laboratory.
Randomize the test scheme to account for potential subjectivity by the analysts.
Prepare the actual test formulations (i.e., perform dilutions) on-site.
Establish a technician team to conduct all tests.
Conduct practice runs in advance of testing to ensure analyst proficiency.
Obtain the randomized order of testing of chemicals from the Study Director.
Initiate testing within 4 weeks after the readiness review, practice/training, and receiving supplies.
Perform testing of three chemicals, three levels each (high, medium, and low), one chemical per day, for three replications.
Include water controls (control carrier counts) each test day for the TSM.
Evaluate each treatment by the TSM, conducting Method 966.04 concurrently on the first replication only.

Dilute Sodium hypochlorite (Sigma Aldrich reagent grade solution, approx. 12% available chlorine, product no. 425044) to 6000 ± 300ppm with reagent grade water, and adjust pH (pH 7.0 ± 0.5) with 5% acetic acid.
Dilute Sodium hypochlorite (Sigma Aldrich reagent grade solution) to 6000 ± 300ppm with reagent grade water, unadjusted pH (pH ~10.0).
Dilute Sodium hypochlorite (Sigma Aldrich reagent grade solution) to 3000 ± 300ppm with reagent grade water, unadjusted pH (pH ~10.0).
Dispense 0.08% peracetic acid/1.0% hydrogen peroxide product.
Activate 2.6% glutaraldehyde product according to product directions.
Use 5 sterile porcelain carriers (only 3 to be used in the assay).
Place a set of 5 carriers into a test tube (25 × 150 mm or 25 × 100 mm) containing test chemical and transfer carriers according to section (g).
Allow carriers to remain in test chemical per the specified contact time and temperature.
Aseptically transfer three of the five carriers individually into tubes containing the neutralizer per section (g).
Transfer each carrier, in sequence, into a tube containing secondary subculture medium as soon as possible.
Inoculate each tube (Neutralizer-Primary and Secondary Subculture treatment tubes) with one mL of each of three inoculum dilutions (10-6, 10-7 and 10-8).
For controls, use three fresh unexposed tubes of neutralizer and three tubes of the secondary subculture medium, and inoculate each control tube with one mL of each of three inoculum dilutions.
Include one uninoculated tube of neutralizer and secondary subculture media to serve as sterility controls.
Incubate all tubes 5-7 days at 36 ± 1ºC.
Record results as growth (+) or no growth (0).
Confirm a minimum of one positive per treatment and control (if available) using Gram staining and colony morphology on TSA.
For each treatment and control group, conduct confirmation testing on growth from the tube with the fewest spores delivered.
Record information and raw data on the test forms and data sheets provided by the Study Director.
Populate electronic spreadsheets with the data from the hard copy data sheets.
Peer-review the electronic spreadsheets for accuracy.
Forward the peer-reviewed spreadsheets to the statistician for analysis.
Record the preparation of media and reagents on Media Preparation Sheets provided by the Study Director.
Substitute 300 at the last dilution for the fraction (A, B, or C).
Substitute 0.5 at the first dilution.
Apply the P/N formula to calculate a log reduction (LR) value consistent with each AOAC test result.
Use the calculated LR values to calculate reproducibility standard deviation (SR) for the AOAC test.
Combine the data across chemicals if the estimates of the variances differ insignificantly.
Run one ANOVA for the test method.
Estimate the mean LR for each test chemical and test method combination using the ANOVA.
Conduct a trend test for each method and disinfectant to determine whether the log spores per treated carrier increases significantly with known efficacy.
Use conventional plots and tests of residuals to check the homogeneous variance and normality assumptions underlying the ANOVA.
Use the Anderson-Darling test to check normality.
Evaluate the % of the total spore counts for each fraction of the TSM and assess the contribution of fraction C for importance.
Clearly describe the selected approach (relative or absolute quantification) and adequately address uncertainties associated with the PCR method.
Estimate and propagate uncertainty throughout data analysis to account for sources of variability.
Establish a sufficient number of controls for the detection of contaminating DNA molecules.
Follow strict protocols to assure that personnel, facilities, workflow, equipment, disposables, negative controls, and laboratory cleaning practices are adequate to avoid contamination.
Follow instrument QC procedures.
Clearly document QA procedures as part of the laboratory's QA program.
Prepare a document describing the PCR method as described in Section 2.5 of the main document.
Predetermine the sensitivity of the assay on different instruments by analyzing common DNA standards.
Make instrument-specific adjustments in thermal cycling parameters or exclude less sensitive instruments from being utilized in the validation study.
Define sensitivity and LOQ characteristics beforehand for each platform and take the differences into consideration in the design of a validation study.

preconditions.text: The analytical method has been developed and optimized.
QC materials (matrix spike or internal control materials) are utilized in a method.
Performance is tested relative to the reference method
Prior to initiation of research, the preparedness of each collaborating laboratory must be assessed.
Readiness review, practice/training, and receiving supplies must be completed.
Randomization of testing order must be completed before experimentation is initiated.
The last carrier has been transferred into the neutralizer tube.
All dilutions produce TNTC (too numerous to count)
The estimates of the variances differ insignificantly among test chemicals.
If large differences in sensitivity with a given reagent are observed
If acceptable minor differences in sensitivity and LOQ are observed and it is economically feasible

constraints.text: A single target organism should be discernible in complex matrices containing potentially millions of non-target organisms.
Test chemicals such as diluted sodium hypochlorite are unstable.
Testing must be completed within 12-14 weeks after initiation of the first test.
Two or three technicians are required per test day for approximately 6 hours.
Randomize any steps in which subjective decisions or unknown factors could affect the conclusions.
Three carriers per treatment for TSM.
Three total tests days per replication; one chemical class tested per day.
Order within a test day will be High, Medium, Low, and Water Control.
pH adjusted treatment must be used in test within approximately 120 min after preparation.
Test within approximately 120 min after preparation.
Must initiate testing within approximately 3 hr after dispensing.
14-day shelf-life post activation. The activated product may be used for testing during the 14-day period.
Carriers must be placed into the test tube containing test chemical within 5 seconds.
The lack of complete neutralization of the disinfectant or bacteriostatic activity of the neutralizer itself may be masked when a high level of inoculum (spores) is added to the subculture tubes.
The uninoculated control tubes must show no growth for the test to be valid.
Standard deviations (Sr and SR) should ideally fall within acceptable ranges (Sr: 0.2 to 1.2, SR: 0.3 to 1.5) to be considered acceptably small.
PCR methods are prone to contamination from extraneous DNA, which can lead to false-positive results.
